rulings.Calendar Matter: Respondents Demurrer To Amended Petition To Return Of Estate Assets Filed By Steven Kmucha
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 5] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Objectors' Demurrer to Amended Petition to Return of Estate Assets Filed by Steven Kumcha [sic]. Objectors' Demurrer to Amended Petition to Return of Estate Assets Filed by Steven Kumcha is OVERRULED. While Objectors present argument in the moving papers regarding the factual allegations presented in the Amended Petition and their own assertion of facts, that is not the relevant analytical exercise on a demurrer. A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 996, 1008, 1009.) A demurrer must demonstrate no right to any relief under the cause of action. (See, Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236.) When considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication. (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal.4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 784.) Neither party has requested the court take judicial notice in support or opposition to this Demurrer. On its own motion, the court takes judicial notice of the January 24, 2024 Order Overruling in Part and Sustaining in Part with Leave to Amend Objectors' Demurrer to Petition to Return of Estate Assets Filed by Steven Kumcha [sic], and the December 9, 2022, Order After Hearing Re Adoption; Setting Hearing On The Petitions; And Directing Service Of This Order, both filed in this case. Objectors demur to the Cause of Action to set aside the 2017 Deed and the Cause of Action for Financial Elder Abuse, on the basis of expired statutes of limitation under Code of Civil Procedure section 343 and Welf. & Inst. Code section 15657.7. The statute of limitations under Code of Civil Procedure section 343, for an action other than the recovery of real property, is four years after the cause of action has accrued. The statute of limitations under Welf. & Inst. Code section 15657.7 for financial elder abuse is "within four years after the plaintiff discovers or, through the exercise of reasonable diligence, should have discovered, the facts constituting the financial abuse." Kmucha, as administrator and in his personal capacity, filed the initial Petition on August 7, 2023, pursuant to Probate Code section 850. (Pet. at par. 21.) Kmucha filed the Amended Petition at issue on this Demurrer on February 9, 2024, pursuant to the January 24, 2024, Order Overruling in Part and Sustaining in Part with Leave to Amend Objectors' Demurrer to Petition to Return of Estate Assets Filed by Steven Kumcha [sic], also pursuant to Probate Code section 850.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 5] 2. Statutes of Limitation Petitioner has brought the Amended Petition in both capacity as Administrator and as an "interested person". (Amd. Pet at 1:17-22, 7:1-24.). The Amended Petition is pleaded in such a way that there is no differentiation between claims brought in Kmucha's individual capacity as an heir or interested person and claims brought in his capacity as Administrator. For the purposes of analysis on this Demurrer brought on the basis of expired statutes of limitation, the court reviews the face of the Amended Petition to determine when Petitioner suspected or had reason to suspect that Objectors had injured either (1) his individual interests, or (2) the Estate's interests, in relation to the gravamen of the action. a. Statutes of Limitation and Kmucha in his Capacity as Administrator The right to sue on a decedent's behalf passes to their personal representative or, if none, the successor in interest, per Code of Civil Procedure sections 377.30, 377.10-377.50 and Welf. & Inst. Code section 15657.3(d)(1). Regarding the Demurrer's argument that an action for financial elder abuse could have been brought by an heir or successor in interest under Welf. & Inst. Code section 15657.3, the Court notes in passing, Qubao Yu filed Petition For Letters Of Administration - on August 29, 2019, at which time the requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 377.32(a) could not have been met (e.g., subd. (a)(3) "The person who seeks to commence an action or proceeding [ ] as the decedent's successor in interest under this article, shall execute and file an affidavit or a declaration under penalty of perjury under the laws of this state stating all of the following; [ ] 'No proceeding is now pending in California for administration of the decedent's estate.'"). Kmucha was appointed Administrator of this estate following a trial to determine, among other issues, the intestate heirs to the Estate of Lambert Genz, and who - between competing petitioners for letters of administration, Steven Kmucha and Quboa Yu - had statutory priority of appointment under Probate Code section 8461. On December 9, 2022, this court issued Order After Hearing Re Adoption; Setting Hearing On The Petitions; And Directing Service Of This Order. That Order, in relevant part, found Kmucha to be Decedent's "next of kin," with priority of appointment as the personal representative of the estate, and his second cousin once removed via a valid adoption. Kmucha's Petition For Letters Of Administration, Appointment Under IAEA was subsequently granted, and Letters of Administration issued April 18, 2023. Kmucha had legal authority to bring an action in his capacity as Administrator as of the date Letters of Administration issued, April 18, 2023. He filed the initial Petition on August 7, 2023, within one year of the Order After Hearing Re Adoption and within four months of the Letters' issuance.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 5] b. Statutes of Limitation and Kmucha in his Capacity as an Individual The Amended Petition alleges Kmucha, personally, "could be said to have had reasonable knowledge of a potential issue [regarding validity of the 2017 Deed] on August 29, 2020." (Amd. Pet. at para. 49.) The Amended Petition also alleges Kmucha was personally aware of the alleged financial elder abuse claim on August 22, 2022. (Amd. Pet. at para. 53.) These allegations are taken as true for the purposes of analysis on demurrer. These potential accrual dates of the pending claims would still be within the statutory periods under Code of Civil Procedure section 343 and Welf. & Inst. Code section 15657.7, as the initial 850 Petition was filed August 7, 2023, fewer than four years after the August 29, 2020 and August 22, 2022 "date of discovery" by Kmucha personally, of these potential claims. c. Allegations Facially Plead Elements of Statutory Tolling During Decedent's Lifetime The Demurrer also argues the relevant statutes of limitation began to accrue during Decedent's lifetime, and ran years before Kmucha was appointed Administrator. On the face of the Amended Petition, Decedent's primary interest was invaded and injured by Objectors' actions in procuring the 2017 Deed. Based on the face of the pleadings, Decedent, or a representative on his behalf, could have brought suit within Decedent's lifetime on the causes of action allegedly arising from the injury sustained in 2017. The four-year statutory period would have run in 2021. However, the Amended Petition alleges Decedent lacked legal capacity during the alleged injury and did not regain legal capacity before his death on March 21, 2019. These allegations go to potential tolling of applicable statutes of limitation during Decedent's lifetime due to a period of disability (see, Code of Civil Procedure sections 328, 352). The court will not make a determination of Decedent's legal capacity or lack of capacity in ruling on this Demurrer. However, in considering a demurrer, the court takes as true all the ultimate facts properly noticed and alleged in the pleading, regardless of how improbable or unlikely such facts might seem. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal. 3d 311, 318; Serrano v. Priest (1971) 5 Cal. 3d 584, 591; see also Del E. Webb Corp. v. Structural Materials Co. (1981) 123 Cal. App. 3d 593, 604). Petitioner sufficiently alleges the factual elements of a lack of "the legal capacity to make decisions" as the term is used in the cited tolling statutes and case law - i.e. "a condition of mental derangement which renders the sufferer incapable of caring for his property or transacting business, or understanding the nature or effects of his acts." (See, Amd. Pet. at paras. 6-13, 15, 79, 80, 88, 89, 91, 92, 97, 112, 123; see also, e.g., Hsu v. Mt. Zion Hosp. (1968) 259 Cal.App.2d 562, 571; Alcott Rehabilitation Hosp. v. Sup.Ct. (Smith) (2001) 93 CA4th 94; Tzolov v. Int'l Jet Leasing, Inc. (1991) 232 Cal.App.3d 117, 118; Pearl v. Pearl (1918) 177 Cal. 303.) 3. Conclusion The Amended Petition's causes of action to set aside the 2017 Deed and for financial elder abuse survive the Demurrer brought on the basis of statutes of limitation under Code of Civil Procedure section 343 and Welf. & Inst. Code section 15657.7.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 5] 1. Determining When a Cause of Action Accrues As discussed by the California Supreme Court in Norgart v. Upjohn Co. (1999) 21 Cal. 4th 383, The general rule for defining the accrual of a cause of action sets the date as the time "when, under the substantive law, the wrongful act is done," or the wrongful result occurs, and the consequent "liability arises...." [citation.] In other words, it sets the date as the time when the cause of action is complete with all of its elements [citations] - the elements being generically referred to by sets of terms such as "wrongdoing" or "wrongful conduct," "cause" or "causation," and "harm" or "injury" [citations]. An exception to the general rule for defining the accrual of a cause of action - indeed, the "most important" one - is the discovery rule. [citation.] It may be expressed by the Legislature or implied by the courts. [citation.] It postpones accrual of a cause of action until the plaintiff discovers, or has reason to discover, the cause of action. [citations.] Id. at 397-99. Further, and specifically in the Probate Code section 850 quiet title action context, "Where the facts adequately allege breach of fiduciary duty or undue influence, the courts will allow a date-of-discovery rule to be applied, " 'when strict adherence to the date of injury rule would result in unfairness to the plaintiff and would encourage wrongdoers to mislead their fiduciary to delay bringing suit. It is particularly appropriate when the defendant maintains custody and control of a plaintiff's property or interests.' [Citation.]" (Estate of Young (2008) 160 Cal. App. 4th 62, 77.) Further still, the statutory limitation and accrual language of Welf. & Inst. Code section 15657.7, for an action based on financial elder abuse, is also based on the time of discovery of the injury, rather than the time of the alleged wrongful conduct. "An action for damages pursuant to Sections 15657.5 and 15657.6 for financial abuse of an elder or dependent adult, as defined in Section 15610.30, shall be commenced within four years after the plaintiff discovers or, through the exercise of reasonable diligence, should have discovered, the facts constituting the financial abuse." The gravamen of a petition brought under Probate Code section 850, and thus the applicable statute(s) of limitation, depends on the factual allegations of the claim. The Amended Petition seeks an order returning the real property to the Estate. Relevant to the gravamen of the Amended Petition's causes of action, and statute of limitations under Welf. & Inst. C. section 15657.7 or Code of Civil Proc. 343, the Petition facially alleges the 2017 Grant Deed at issue is invalid due to Decedent's lack of capacity at relevant and specifically pleaded times, and is invalid because it was procured by undue influence and/or financial elder abuse of Decedent by Objectors. The Amended Petition facially alleges Objectors were in a fiduciary relationship with Decedent at the relevant times, and maintained custody and control of both Decedent and the real property at all relevant times. On the basis of the Amended Petition's allegations, the court could appropriately allow the "date of discovery rule" to define accrual of the causes of action seeking to invalidate the 2017 Grant Deed on the basis of undue influence and financial elder abuse.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 5 of 5] The Amended Petition pleads specific material facts that, taken as true for the purposes of analysis on demurrer, support the applicable claims regarding when the causes of action accrued, based on injury to the Estate or to Kmucha as an interested person and/or heir. As to accrual of the relevant statutes of limitation accruing within Decedent's lifetime, the Amended Petition alleges sufficient facts to support statutory tolling due to Decedent's alleged legal incapacity. Whether Petitioner will be able to support these claims with sufficient evidence, and whether Objectors will be able to rebut or defend against these claims with their own showing of sufficient evidence, is not before the court on a demurrer. Petitioner Kmucha shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.
